title: The king is slow
summary: An English grandfather teaches his nine-year-old granddaughter her first chess lesson. She likes the pawns. He explains why the most important piece is not the strongest.

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: Ivy, sit here. I see you have a question.

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: You stood at the door for one minute. That is your question face.

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: Good. The board has sixty-four squares. Look.

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: Now look at the pieces. Eight small ones in the front. These are pawns. They walk one step at a time. They are not strong, but they are many.

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: Most children like the queen first. The queen is strong.

body.speaker: Ivy
body.text: Grandpa, what is the most important piece?

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: The king. He is slow. He walks one step. But if you lose the king, the game is over. The strongest is not the most important. Remember that.

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: Yes. That is why I love it.

body.speaker: Ivy
body.text: Grandpa, may I move a pawn?

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: Yes. The white pawn. Two steps forward, only for the first move.

body.speaker: Mr Halford
body.text: Like this. Now I move. We are playing.

annotations.meaningVi: từng bước một (cụm từ cố định)
gương mặt tò mò/muốn hỏi (cụm danh từ ghép)
nhớ lấy điều đó (lời nhắc nhở)
con có thể đi (hỏi xin phép lịch sự với 'may')

grammarNotes.title: Superlatives: 'the strongest', 'the most important'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để so sánh ở mức cao nhất, A1 dùng hai cách: với tính từ ngắn — 'the + adj + -est' ('the strongest', 'the slowest'); với tính từ dài — 'the most + adj' ('the most important'). Trong bài: 'What is the most important piece?' 'The strongest is not the most important.' Dùng cùng 'the' phía trước. Lưu ý 'first' trong 'like the queen first' không phải superlative — nó là trạng từ chỉ thứ tự.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Two superlative forms: short adjectives — 'the + adj + -est' ('the strongest', 'the slowest'); long adjectives — 'the most + adj' ('the most important'). The dialogue: 'What is the most important piece?' 'The strongest is not the most important.' Both forms take 'the' in front. Note: 'first' in 'like the queen first' is not a superlative — it is a sequence adverb.

translationVi:
Ông Halford: Ivy, ngồi đây. Ông thấy con có câu hỏi.
Ivy: Sao ông biết?
Ông Halford: Con đứng ở cửa một phút. Đó là gương mặt khi con muốn hỏi.
Ivy: Con muốn học cờ vua.
Ông Halford: Tốt. Bàn cờ có sáu mươi tư ô. Nhìn này.
Ivy: Con thấy rồi.
Ông Halford: Giờ nhìn các quân. Tám quân nhỏ ở hàng trước. Đây là quân tốt. Chúng đi một bước một lần. Chúng không mạnh, nhưng chúng nhiều.
Ivy: Con thích quân tốt.
Ông Halford: Phần lớn trẻ con thích quân hậu trước. Quân hậu mạnh.
Ivy: Ông ơi, quân nào quan trọng nhất?
Ông Halford: Không phải quân hậu.
Ivy: Quân xe?
Ông Halford: Không phải xe.
Ivy: Quân tượng?
Ông Halford: Không phải tượng.
Ivy: Ông nói đi.
Ông Halford: Quân vua. Vua chậm. Vua đi một bước. Nhưng nếu con mất vua, ván cờ kết thúc. Quân mạnh nhất không phải quân quan trọng nhất. Nhớ đó.
Ivy: Trò chơi lạ thật.
Ông Halford: Đúng. Vì thế ông yêu nó.
Ivy: Ông ơi, con đi một quân tốt được không?
Ông Halford: Được. Quân tốt trắng. Hai bước về phía trước, chỉ lần đầu.
Ivy: Vầy này ạ?
Ông Halford: Vầy. Giờ ông đi. Mình đang chơi.

questions.prompt: How does Mr Halford know Ivy has a question?
options: She stood at the door for one minute — her 'question face.'

questions.prompt: How many squares does a chessboard have?

options: One step at a time; they are not strong, but they are many
questions.explanation: 'They walk one step at a time. They are not strong, but they are many.'

questions.prompt: Which piece do most children like first, according to Mr Halford?
questions.hint: Mr Halford's comment about other children.

questions.prompt: What is the most important piece?
questions.explanation: 'The king. He is slow. He walks one step. But if you lose the king, the game is over.'

questions.prompt: What lesson does Mr Halford want Ivy to remember?
options: 'The strongest is not the most important.'

questions.prompt: What does Ivy call the game?
How many squares can the white pawn move on its first move?
options: Two squares forward (only on the first move)
questions.explanation: 'Two steps forward, only for the first move.'

options: Ivy makes her first move; Mr Halford moves; 'We are playing.'
Mr Halford explains all the pieces in detail.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Strength and importance are not the same thing — and a child's quiet preference (for pawns or for a slow king) may already point at something real.
Chess is too hard for children.
questions.explanation: Mr Halford insists 'the strongest is not the most important.'
questions.hint: Think about Mr Halford's main teaching point.

cloze.template:
Mr Halford: Ivy, sit here. I see you have a question.
Ivy: How did you know?
Mr Halford: You stood at the door for one {{b1}}. That is your question face.
Ivy: I want to learn chess.
Mr Halford: Good. The board has sixty-four {{b2}}. Look.
Ivy: I see them.
Mr Halford: Now look at the pieces. Eight small ones in the front. These are {{b3}}. They walk one step at a time. They are not strong, but they are many.
Ivy: I like the pawns.
Mr Halford: Most children like the {{b4}} first. The queen is strong.
Ivy: Grandpa, what is the most {{b5}} piece?
Mr Halford: It is not the queen.
Ivy: The rook?
Mr Halford: Not the rook.
Ivy: The bishop?
Mr Halford: Not the bishop.
Ivy: Tell me.
Mr Halford: The {{b6}}. He is slow. He walks one step. But if you lose the king, the game is over. The strongest is not the most important. Remember that.
Ivy: It is a {{b7}} game.
Mr Halford: Yes. That is why I love it.
Ivy: Grandpa, may I move a pawn?
Mr Halford: Yes. The white pawn. {{b8}} steps forward, only for the first move.
Ivy: Like this?
Mr Halford: Like this. Now I {{b9}}. We are {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: Eight pawns in the front row.
The king is the most important.
Two steps for the first move.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Halford says 'the strongest is not the most important.' In a place you know well — your family, your team, your class — who or what is the strongest, and who or what is actually the most important? Are they the same?
